canonical_examples.analysis: SDT separates the listener's sensitivity from willingness to say 'yes.' Hits and false alarms are both required: a high hit rate alone may just reflect a liberal criterion. By comparing hit rate to false-alarm rate, the analyst estimates d-prime as discriminability and beta or criterion c as response bias. The same auditory system can produce different yes/no rates when payoffs or prior probabilities move the criterion.
canonical_examples.problem: In the classic psychophysics experiment, a listener hears many trials, some containing a faint tone in noise and some containing noise alone, and must answer whether the tone was present.

canonical_examples.analysis: Tanner and Swets treated the physician as a detector facing overlapping distributions: diseased and non-diseased patients can generate similar evidence. Raising the decision threshold reduces false positives but increases misses; lowering it increases sensitivity to disease but creates more false alarms. ROC analysis evaluates the diagnostic information in the test separately from the clinician's operating point, making the threshold tradeoff explicit.
canonical_examples.problem: Early SDT work on medical diagnosis asks how a physician should interpret ambiguous clinical or radiographic evidence when both missed disease and false diagnosis are costly.

core_claims: Observed accuracy cannot be interpreted without separating discriminability from response criterion.
A detector can increase hits by adopting a liberal criterion, but this necessarily tends to increase false alarms when signal and noise distributions overlap.
False alarms are not mere errors to discard; they are essential evidence for estimating sensitivity.
ROC curves characterize the tradeoff across criteria and allow comparison of detectors independently of any single threshold.
Optimal response criterion depends on base rates and payoff costs, not only on perceptual or informational quality.
Two systems with the same percent correct can have different d-prime values and therefore different underlying sensitivity.
Detection problems are fundamentally about classifying noisy evidence generated by overlapping signal-present and signal-absent distributions.
description: Signal Detection Theory analyzes decisions under perceptual or informational uncertainty by separating sensitivity to signal from response bias, using hits, misses, false alarms, correct rejections, criteria, and ROC curves.
failure_modes: Collapsing the analysis into accuracy or precision and losing the central distinction between discriminability and criterion.
Assuming the equal-variance normal model when empirical signal and noise distributions are skewed, unequal, or multi-modal.
Treating the mathematically optimal criterion as ethically or operationally acceptable when false positives and misses are borne by different people.
Ignoring base-rate shift: a criterion tuned in one environment can perform poorly when prevalence changes.
Practitioners may overfit ROC performance to a benchmark dataset and miss how real-world incentives change the operating point.
Using SDT language for cases where there is no defensible ground truth or stable signal/noise distinction.

key_vocabulary.Correct rejection: A noise-only trial on which the detector correctly says the signal is absent.
key_vocabulary.Criterion: The decision threshold on the evidence axis above which the detector says the signal is present.
key_vocabulary.False alarm: A noise-only trial on which the detector says the signal is present.
key_vocabulary.Hit: A signal-present trial on which the detector correctly says the signal is present.
key_vocabulary.Miss: A signal-present trial on which the detector says the signal is absent.
key_vocabulary.ROC curve: Receiver operating characteristic curve: the plot of hit rate against false-alarm rate across possible criteria.
key_vocabulary.Response bias: A tendency toward liberal or conservative responses independent of sensory sensitivity, often shaped by priors and payoffs.
key_vocabulary.Signal and noise distributions: The probability distributions of internal evidence when the signal is present or absent; overlap creates classification uncertainty.
key_vocabulary.d-prime: A measure of sensitivity or discriminability, usually the standardized distance between signal and noise distributions.

name: Signal Detection Theory
operative_procedure: Define the binary state of the world: signal present versus signal absent, disease versus no disease, threat versus no threat, or another defensible pair.
Define the binary response or action: say yes versus no, flag versus pass, treat versus do not treat.
Build the confusion matrix: count hits, misses, false alarms, and correct rejections on cases with known ground truth.
Compute hit rate and false-alarm rate, using appropriate corrections when rates are 0 or 1.
Estimate discriminability, commonly d-prime, to ask how well the evidence separates signal from noise independent of response tendency.
Estimate criterion or response bias to ask whether the detector is liberal or conservative relative to the evidence distributions.
Plot or inspect the ROC curve when thresholds vary, and compare detectors by curve position or area rather than one threshold's accuracy alone.
Set the operating criterion using base rates, costs of misses, costs of false alarms, and capacity constraints.
Report both sensitivity and criterion, and state how the recommendation would change if prevalence or payoff structure changed.
scope_and_limits: Signal Detection Theory is strongest when ground truth can be labeled, responses can be counted, and errors have asymmetric costs. It is useful in perception, diagnostics, screening, security, forecasting, and binary classifiers. It is weaker when categories are unstable, values cannot be reduced to hit and false-alarm tradeoffs, or social consequences of errors dominate the statistical operating point.
source_summary: This skill was authored from model knowledge of Green and Swets, Tanner and Swets, Macmillan and Creelman, and ROC/psychophysics literature, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: David M. Green and John A. Swets, Signal Detection Theory and Psychophysics
Wilson P. Tanner Jr. and John A. Swets, 'A Decision-Making Theory of Visual Detection'
Neil A. Macmillan and C. Douglas Creelman, Detection Theory: A User's Guide

when_to_use: a classifier, judge, doctor, sensor, model, or analyst must distinguish signal from noise under uncertainty
a reported accuracy rate hides whether errors come from poor sensitivity or from an overly liberal or conservative threshold
false positives and false negatives have different costs and the decision threshold must be chosen explicitly
performance must be compared across tests, detectors, or models without being fooled by different operating criteria
base rates or incentives changed and an old threshold now produces too many misses or false alarms
